---
title: "thesis_testing"
author: "Elias Ovesen"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
1 + 1
```

You can add options to executable code like this

```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# 1. SETUP & LIBRARIES
# ==============================================================================
personal_lib <- "~/R/library"
if (!dir.exists(personal_lib)) dir.create(personal_lib, recursive = TRUE)
.libPaths(c(personal_lib, .libPaths()))

required_packages <- c("rstan", "tidyverse", "nadiv", "MCMCglmm") 
for (pkg in required_packages) {
  if (!require(pkg, character.only = TRUE, quietly = TRUE)) {
    install.packages(pkg, lib = personal_lib, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)
  }
}

# ==============================================================================
# 2. LOAD & CLEAN DATA (OPTIMIZED ORDER)
# ==============================================================================

# Pedigree
pedData <- read.csv("pedigree_data.txt", sep = " ", header = TRUE, na.strings = "NA")
pedigree_clean <- pedData %>%
  dplyr::select(ringnr, dam, sire) %>%
  mutate(across(c(ringnr, dam, sire), ~ as.character(trimws(gsub("_.*$", "", .)))),
         dam = if_else(is.na(dam) | dam == "" | dam == "NA", NA_character_, dam),
         sire = if_else(is.na(sire) | sire == "" | sire == "NA", NA_character_, sire))

# External Parents
all_parents <- unique(c(pedigree_clean$dam, pedigree_clean$sire))
external_parents <- setdiff(all_parents, c(pedigree_clean$ringnr, NA_character_))
if (length(external_parents) > 0) {
  founder_df <- data.frame(ringnr = external_parents, dam = NA_character_, sire = NA_character_)
  pedigree_full <- bind_rows(founder_df, pedigree_clean) %>% distinct(ringnr, .keep_all = TRUE)
} else {
  pedigree_full <- pedigree_clean
}

pedigree_prepped <- prepPed(pedigree_full, gender = NULL, check = TRUE)

# Trait Data
traitData <- read.csv("morphology.txt", sep = ";", header = TRUE, 
                      na.strings = c("NA", ""), fileEncoding = "Windows-1252", 
                      stringsAsFactors = FALSE, colClasses = c(ringnr = "character"))

# A. LOAD & LIGHT FORMATTING (Crucial for matching)
trait_raw <- traitData %>%
  filter(sted_r == "hestmannøy", 
         !is.na(ving_h), !is.na(nebb_l), !is.na(tars_h), !is.na(nebb_h), !is.na(vekt)) %>%
  mutate(ringnr = as.character(trimws(gsub("_.*$", "", ringnr))))

# B. ALIGN WITH PEDIGREE (The "Gatekeeper" Step)
valid_ids <- intersect(trait_raw$ringnr, pedigree_prepped$ringnr)
trait_subset_temp <- trait_raw %>% filter(ringnr %in% valid_ids)

cat("Birds in Raw File:    ", nrow(trait_raw), "\n")
cat("Birds with Pedigree:  ", nrow(trait_subset_temp), "\n")

# C. DEEP CLEANING (Sex, Age, Bad IDs) on the SUBSET
bad_ids <- c("8N27558", "8N42527", "8N13933", "8M71874", "8N87712", "8L89527")

trait_subset_temp <- trait_subset_temp %>%
  filter(!ringnr %in% bad_ids) %>%
  mutate(raw_sex = if_else(!is.na(scriptsex) & scriptsex != "u", scriptsex, fieldsex)) %>%
  mutate(sex = case_when(
    raw_sex %in% c("m", "pm") ~ "m", 
    raw_sex %in% c("f", "pf") ~ "f", 
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  )) %>%
  filter(!is.na(sex)) %>%
  mutate(
    s_age = trimws(as.character(scriptage)),
    f_age = trimws(as.character(fieldage)),
    age_class = case_when(
      grepl("^1K", s_age) ~ "juvenile",
      grepl("^[2-9]K", s_age) | grepl("^1[0-9]K", s_age) ~ "adult",
      f_age %in% c("j", "pj") ~ "juvenile",
      f_age %in% c("a", "pa") ~ "adult",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_class))

# D. STATISTICAL OUTLIER REMOVAL (Residual Check)
outlier_ids_to_kill <- c()
traits_to_check <- c("ving_h", "tars_h", "nebb_l", "nebb_h", "vekt")

for(t in traits_to_check) {
  f <- as.formula(paste(t, "~ sex + age_class"))
  fit <- lm(f, data = trait_subset_temp, na.action = na.exclude)
  
  resids <- rstudent(fit)
  cutoff <- if(t == "vekt") 6.0 else 4.0 
  
  bad_rows <- which(abs(resids) > cutoff)
  
  if(length(bad_rows) > 0) {
    new_bad_ids <- trait_subset_temp$ringnr[bad_rows]
    outlier_ids_to_kill <- c(outlier_ids_to_kill, new_bad_ids)
    cat("Trait:", t, "- Removing", length(new_bad_ids), "IDs\n")
  }
}

# E. FINAL DATASET
trait_subset_lv <- trait_subset_temp %>%
  filter(!ringnr %in% unique(outlier_ids_to_kill))

cat("Final Clean N: ", nrow(trait_subset_lv), "\n")



# NEW: Log-transform FIRST, then scale
trait_subset_lv <- trait_subset_lv %>%
  mutate(
    ving_h_std = as.numeric(scale(log(ving_h))),
    nebb_l_std = as.numeric(scale(log(nebb_l))),
    tars_h_std = as.numeric(scale(log(tars_h))),
    nebb_h_std = as.numeric(scale(log(nebb_h))),
    vekt_std   = as.numeric(scale(log(vekt)))
  )


cat("Data Loaded. N Rows:", nrow(trait_subset_lv), "\n")




# 1. Convert to Date (R handles YYYY-MM-DD automatically)
trait_subset_lv$date_obj <- as.Date(trait_subset_lv$dato)

# 2. Extract month
trait_subset_lv$month <- as.numeric(format(trait_subset_lv$date_obj, "%m"))



# 1. Extract the Year as a factor
trait_subset_lv <- trait_subset_lv %>%
  mutate(
    Year = factor(format(date_obj, "%Y")),
    # Create the 3-level Season we discussed
    Season_3 = factor(case_when(
      month %in% c(5, 6, 7) ~ "Breeding",
      month %in% c(8, 9)    ~ "Molt",
      TRUE                  ~ "Winter"
    ))
  )






# Grouping low-volume measurers using case_when
trait_subset_lv <- trait_subset_lv %>%
  group_by(init) %>%
  mutate(init_clean = case_when(
    n() < 10 ~ "OTHER",
    TRUE     ~ as.character(init)
  )) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(init_clean = factor(init_clean))


###############################################################

############### DID I NOT ADD YEAR HERE?????? ##################

##############################################################

############# =============================================


# ==============================================================================
# 2.5 LOAD & CLEAN FITNESS DATA
# ==============================================================================

fitness_data <- read.csv("Fitness.csv", header = TRUE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, na.strings = "NA", fileEncoding = "Windows-1252")

fitness_data_clean <- fitness_data %>%
  dplyr::select(Location, Year_ID, Year, Sex, Own_survival, N_Recruits, Least_age) %>%
  filter(grepl("^Hestmann", Location)) %>%
  mutate(
    # Extract the ring number
    ringnr = sub("^.*_", "", Year_ID),
    
    # NEW: Extract the actual calendar year and overwrite the 1-22 index
    Year = sub("_.*$", "", Year_ID), 
    
    Own_survival = as.numeric(Own_survival)
  ) %>%
  # Gatekeeper: Must be in the prepared pedigree
  filter(ringnr %in% pedigree_prepped$ringnr)

# Split into two clean datasets (Stan cannot handle NA in the response variable)
data_repro <- fitness_data_clean %>% drop_na(N_Recruits)
data_surv  <- fitness_data_clean %>% drop_na(Own_survival)

cat("Morphology records: ", nrow(trait_subset_lv), "\n")
cat("Reproduction records: ", nrow(data_repro), "\n")
cat("Survival records: ", nrow(data_surv), "\n")







# ==============================================================================
# 3. PEDIGREE PROCESSING
# ==============================================================================
pedigree_working <- pedigree_prepped
colnames(pedigree_working) <- c("id", "dam", "sire") 
pedigree_working$id   <- as.character(pedigree_working$id)
pedigree_working$dam  <- as.character(pedigree_working$dam)
pedigree_working$sire <- as.character(pedigree_working$sire)

# NEW: Keep all birds that appear in ANY of the three datasets
birds_to_keep <- unique(c(trait_subset_lv$ringnr, data_repro$ringnr, data_surv$ringnr))

ped_pruned <- MCMCglmm::prunePed(pedigree_working, keep = birds_to_keep)

A_inv_obj <- MCMCglmm::inverseA(ped_pruned, nodes = "ALL", scale = FALSE)
ordered_ids <- rownames(A_inv_obj$Ainv)

ped_ordered <- data.frame(
  id = ordered_ids,
  dam = as.character(A_inv_obj$pedigree[, 2]), 
  sire = as.character(A_inv_obj$pedigree[, 3]),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

dii_vector <- A_inv_obj$dii
Na <- nrow(ped_ordered)

dam_idx_mapped  <- match(ped_ordered$dam, ped_ordered$id)
sire_idx_mapped <- match(ped_ordered$sire, ped_ordered$id)

dam_idx_mapped[is.na(dam_idx_mapped)]   <- Na + 1
sire_idx_mapped[is.na(sire_idx_mapped)] <- Na + 1

id_map <- setNames(1:Na, ped_ordered$id)






# ==============================================================================
# 4. DATA MAPPING & UNIVERSAL FACTORS
# ==============================================================================
# 1. Map IDs
animal_morph <- unname(id_map[trait_subset_lv$ringnr])
animal_repro <- unname(id_map[data_repro$ringnr])
animal_surv  <- unname(id_map[data_surv$ringnr])

# 2. Universal Year Map (Crucial so Year 1 is the same everywhere)
all_years <- sort(unique(c(as.character(trait_subset_lv$Year), 
                           as.character(data_repro$Year), 
                           as.character(data_surv$Year))))
year_map <- setNames(1:length(all_years), all_years)

year_morph <- unname(year_map[as.character(trait_subset_lv$Year)])
year_repro <- unname(year_map[as.character(data_repro$Year)])
year_surv  <- unname(year_map[as.character(data_surv$Year)])

# 3. Measurer Map
init_factor <- as.integer(factor(trait_subset_lv$init_clean))

# 4. Trait Matrix
Y_mat <- as.matrix(trait_subset_lv[, c("tars_h_std", "ving_h_std", "nebb_l_std", "nebb_h_std", "vekt_std")])




# ==============================================================================
# 5. STAN EXECUTION FOR JOINT MODEL
# ==============================================================================
dataset_joint <- list(
  # Dimensions
  No_morph = nrow(trait_subset_lv),
  No_repro = nrow(data_repro),
  No_surv  = nrow(data_surv),
  Nt = 5, 
  Nlv = 1,
  Na = Na,
  
  # Responses
  Y_morph = Y_mat,
  Y_repro = as.array(as.integer(data_repro$N_Recruits)),
  Y_surv  = as.array(as.integer(data_surv$Own_survival)),
  
  # Animal Indices
  animal_morph = as.array(as.integer(animal_morph)),
  animal_repro = as.array(as.integer(animal_repro)),
  animal_surv  = as.array(as.integer(animal_surv)),
  
  # Pedigree
  dam  = as.array(as.integer(dam_idx_mapped)), 
  sire = as.array(as.integer(sire_idx_mapped)),
  dii  = as.array(dii_vector),
  
  # Random Effects Indices
  N_year = length(all_years),
  year_morph = as.array(as.integer(year_morph)),
  year_repro = as.array(as.integer(year_repro)),
  year_surv  = as.array(as.integer(year_surv)),
  
  N_init = max(init_factor),
  init_morph = as.array(as.integer(init_factor))
)

rstan_options(auto_write = TRUE)
options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())


init_fn_joint <- function() {
  list(
    # Core Morphology (Anchored for 1 LV)
    lambda_anchor = array(runif(dataset_joint$Nlv, 0.5, 1.2)), 
    lambda_free = matrix(rnorm((dataset_joint$Nt - 1) * dataset_joint$Nlv, 0, 0.1), 
                         nrow = dataset_joint$Nt - 1, ncol = dataset_joint$Nlv),
    rho = runif(1, 0.4, 0.6),        
    h2_lv = array(runif(dataset_joint$Nlv, 0.4, 0.6)),      
    sd_R = runif(dataset_joint$Nt, 0.5, 1.0), 
    
    # Intercepts & Slopes (Fitness gradients as arrays of length 1)
    alpha_trait = rnorm(dataset_joint$Nt, 0, 0.5),
    alpha_repro = rnorm(1, -0.5, 0.5),
    alpha_surv  = rnorm(1, 0, 0.5),
    gamma_repro = array(rnorm(dataset_joint$Nlv, 0, 0.1)),
    gamma_surv  = array(rnorm(dataset_joint$Nlv, 0, 0.1)),
    
    # Genetic Noise (Now a 1 x Na matrix)
    w_a = matrix(rnorm(dataset_joint$Nlv * dataset_joint$Na, 0, 0.1), 
                 nrow = dataset_joint$Nlv),
    w_pe = matrix(rnorm(dataset_joint$Nlv * dataset_joint$Na, 0, 0.1), 
                  nrow = dataset_joint$Nlv),
    
    # Random Effects
    sd_year_morph = runif(dataset_joint$Nt, 0.05, 0.2),
    sd_year_repro = runif(1, 0.05, 0.2),
    sd_year_surv  = runif(1, 0.05, 0.2),
    sd_init_morph = runif(dataset_joint$Nt, 0.05, 0.2),
    
    z_year_morph = matrix(rnorm(dataset_joint$N_year * dataset_joint$Nt, 0, 0.1), 
                          nrow = dataset_joint$N_year),
    z_year_repro = rnorm(dataset_joint$N_year, 0, 0.1),
    z_year_surv  = rnorm(dataset_joint$N_year, 0, 0.1),
    z_init_morph = matrix(rnorm(dataset_joint$N_init * dataset_joint$Nt, 0, 0.1), 
                          nrow = dataset_joint$N_init)
  )
}

# When calling stan(), remember to use this updated init:
# init = init_fn_joint_1lv




# Run the Joint Model
out_joint <- stan(
  file = "combined_model_test.stan",
  data = dataset_joint,
  init = init_fn_joint,        
  chains = 4, 
  pars = c("alpha_trait", "alpha_repro", "alpha_surv", 
           "gamma_repro", "gamma_surv", "Lambda", "sd_R", 
           "sd_year_morph", "sd_year_repro", "sd_year_surv", "sd_init_morph", 
           "h2_lv"
           # , "rho"
           ),
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95), 
  iter = 6000,          
  warmup = 2000,
  sample_file = "output_joint_chain"
)

saveRDS(out_joint, 'Output_Joint_Starter.rds')

















# ==============================================================================
# 6. RESULTS & DIAGNOSTICS
# ==============================================================================
out_lv <- readRDS('output_markov/Output_Joint_Starter.rds')

# Print the parameters specific to the Joint Starter model
print(out_lv,
      pars = c("alpha_trait",       # Base intercepts for the 5 morph traits
               "alpha_repro",       # Base intercept for reproduction
               "alpha_surv",        # Base intercept for survival
               "gamma_repro",       # Selection gradient: Size -> Reproduction!
               "gamma_surv",        # Selection gradient: Size -> Survival!
               "Lambda",            # Factor loadings (How traits build Size)
               "h2_lv",             # Heritability of the Latent Size
               # "rho",               # Shrinkage factor
               "sd_R",              # Residual error for morph traits
               "sd_year_morph",     # Year variance for morphology
               "sd_year_repro",     # Year variance for reproduction
               "sd_year_surv",      # Year variance for survival
               "sd_init_morph",     # Measurer variance for morphology
               "lp__"),             # Log-posterior (model fit)
      probs = c(0.025, 0.975),
      digits_summary = 3)





library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Plot a stable parameter (The Grand Mean)
color_scheme_set("mix-blue-red")
p1 <- mcmc_trace(out_lv, pars = c("alpha_trait[1]", "sd_year_surv")) +
  ggtitle("Stable Parameters (Rhat = 1.00)")

# 2. Plot the 'Flipped' parameters
p2 <- mcmc_trace(out_lv, pars = c("Lambda[1,1]", "gamma_repro[1]", "gamma_surv[1]")) +
  ggtitle("Sign-Flipped Parameters (High Rhat)")

p3 <- mcmc_trace(out_lv, pars = c("Lambda[1,2]", "Lambda[3,2]", "gamma_repro[2]", "gamma_surv[2]"))
  

# Show the plots
print(p1)
print(p2)
print(p3)







```

A quick check here showed that there were quite a few birds that were phenotyped but that were not in the fitness data, so that we are missing about 30 percent of the measurements if we only select birds that are in all data sets. So, the solution here might be to keep all phenotyped birds, but only have fitness data for the ones that have that, and that is possible to do as far as I know. 


```{stan}
data {
  // Dimensions
  int<lower=1> No_morph; 
  int<lower=1> No_repro;
  int<lower=1> No_surv;
  int<lower=1> Nt;  
  int<lower=1> Nlv; 
  int<lower=1> Na; 
  
  // Responses
  matrix[No_morph, Nt] Y_morph; 
  array[No_repro] int<lower=0> Y_repro; // Poisson count
  array[No_surv] int<lower=0, upper=1> Y_surv; // Bernoulli 0/1
  
  // Pedigree & Links
  array[No_morph] int<lower=1, upper=Na> animal_morph; 
  array[No_repro] int<lower=1, upper=Na> animal_repro;
  array[No_surv]  int<lower=1, upper=Na> animal_surv;
  
  array[Na] int<lower=1, upper=Na+1> dam; 
  array[Na] int<lower=1, upper=Na+1> sire;
  vector[Na] dii; 
  
  // Random Effects Indices
  int<lower=1> N_year;
  array[No_morph] int<lower=1, upper=N_year> year_morph;
  array[No_repro] int<lower=1, upper=N_year> year_repro;
  array[No_surv]  int<lower=1, upper=N_year> year_surv;
  
  int<lower=1> N_init;
  array[No_morph] int<lower=1, upper=N_init> init_morph;
}

parameters {
  // Intercepts (Since we dropped the X matrix)
  vector[Nt] alpha_trait;
  real alpha_repro;
  real alpha_surv;

  // Selection Gradients (The link between Size and Fitness!)
  vector[Nlv] gamma_repro;
  vector[Nlv] gamma_surv;

  // Variances
  vector<lower=0>[Nt] sd_R; 
  // real<lower=0, upper=1> rho; 
  vector<lower=0, upper=1>[Nlv] h2_lv; 
  matrix[Nt, Nlv] lambda_raw;

  // Latent Effects
  matrix[Nlv, Na] w_a;  
  matrix[Nlv, Na] w_pe; 
  
  // Random Effects (Year and Measurer)
  matrix[N_year, Nt] z_year_morph;
  vector<lower=0>[Nt] sd_year_morph;
  
  vector[N_year] z_year_repro;
  real<lower=0> sd_year_repro;
  
  vector[N_year] z_year_surv;
  real<lower=0> sd_year_surv;
  
  matrix[N_init, Nt] z_init_morph;
  vector<lower=0>[Nt] sd_init_morph;
}

transformed parameters {
  // 1. Pedigree Recursion
  matrix[Na, Nlv] G_effect; 
  {
    matrix[Na+1, Nlv] aug_a;
    aug_a[Na+1] = rep_row_vector(0, Nlv);
    for (i in 1:Na) {
       aug_a[i] = 0.5 * aug_a[dam[i]] + 0.5 * aug_a[sire[i]] 
                  + sqrt(dii[i]) * w_a[, i]'; 
    }
    G_effect = aug_a[1:Na];
  }

  // 2. Latent Factor
  matrix[Na, Nlv] LV;
  for(k in 1:Nlv) {
    LV[, k] = sqrt(h2_lv[k]) * G_effect[, k] + sqrt(1 - h2_lv[k]) * w_pe[k, ]';
  }

  // 3. Shrinkage
  matrix[Nt, Nlv] Lambda;
  Lambda[, 1] = lambda_raw[, 1];            
  // Lambda[, 2] = lambda_raw[, 2] * sqrt(rho); 
}

model {
  // Priors
  // rho ~ beta(1.7, 2.5);
  h2_lv ~ beta(2, 2); 
  to_vector(lambda_raw) ~ std_normal();
  sd_R ~ normal(0, 1);
  
  alpha_trait ~ normal(0, 2);
  alpha_repro ~ normal(0, 2);
  alpha_surv ~ normal(0, 2);
  
  gamma_repro ~ normal(0, 1);
  gamma_surv ~ normal(0, 1);

  to_vector(w_a) ~ std_normal();
  to_vector(w_pe) ~ std_normal();
  to_vector(z_year_morph) ~ std_normal();
  z_year_repro ~ std_normal();
  z_year_surv ~ std_normal();
  to_vector(z_init_morph) ~ std_normal();
  
  sd_year_morph ~ exponential(2); 
  sd_year_repro ~ exponential(2); 
  sd_year_surv ~ exponential(2); 
  sd_init_morph ~ exponential(2);

  // --- LIKELIHOODS ---
  
  // 1. Morphology
  for(i in 1:No_morph){
    for(t in 1:Nt){
      real mu = alpha_trait[t] 
                + LV[animal_morph[i], 1] * Lambda[t, 1] 
                // + LV[animal_morph[i], 2] * Lambda[t, 2]
                + z_year_morph[year_morph[i], t] * sd_year_morph[t]
                + z_init_morph[init_morph[i], t] * sd_init_morph[t];
      Y_morph[i, t] ~ normal(mu, sd_R[t]);
    }
  }
  

  for(i in 1:No_repro){
    real log_lambda = alpha_repro 
                      + gamma_repro[1] * LV[animal_repro[i], 1] 
                      + z_year_repro[year_repro[i]] * sd_year_repro;
    Y_repro[i] ~ poisson_log(log_lambda);
  }
  
  // 3. Survival (Bernoulli)
  for(i in 1:No_surv){
    real logit_p = alpha_surv 
                   + gamma_surv[1] * LV[animal_surv[i], 1] 
                   + z_year_surv[year_surv[i]] * sd_year_surv;
    Y_surv[i] ~ bernoulli_logit(logit_p);
  }
}



```



This run with 1 latent variable on the 5 traits, with survival and reproduction added led to these successful results (this was without all the fixed and random effects by the way):

:::{.output}
Chain 4:                16775.2 seconds (Total)
Chain 4:
Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
4 chains, each with iter=6000; warmup=2000; thin=1;
post-warmup draws per chain=4000, total post-warmup draws=16000.

                      mean se_mean     sd      2.5%     97.5% n_eff  Rhat
alpha_trait[1]      -0.022   0.001  0.057    -0.131     0.091  4522 1.001
alpha_trait[2]       0.037   0.001  0.064    -0.089     0.162  4292 1.001
alpha_trait[3]       0.022   0.001  0.065    -0.103     0.150  3924 1.001
alpha_trait[4]       0.202   0.001  0.079     0.050     0.361  3457 1.000
alpha_trait[5]       0.111   0.001  0.071    -0.027     0.250  3974 1.001
alpha_repro         -0.354   0.002  0.100    -0.553    -0.156  2557 1.001
alpha_surv           0.186   0.001  0.085     0.015     0.354  6752 1.000
gamma_repro[1]       0.074   0.000  0.024     0.026     0.122 30770 1.000
gamma_surv[1]        0.115   0.000  0.042     0.033     0.196 30325 1.000
Lambda[1,1]          0.970   0.000  0.020     0.931     1.011  2055 1.003
Lambda[2,1]          0.226   0.000  0.015     0.196     0.255 13184 1.000
Lambda[3,1]          0.158   0.000  0.015     0.129     0.187 21086 1.000
Lambda[4,1]          0.119   0.000  0.015     0.091     0.149 24667 1.000
Lambda[5,1]          0.316   0.000  0.016     0.286     0.347  8836 1.001
h2_lv[1]             0.472   0.001  0.046     0.382     0.559  1366 1.003
sd_R[1]              0.189   0.000  0.003     0.184     0.194  9801 1.000
sd_R[2]              0.905   0.000  0.010     0.886     0.924 35016 1.000
sd_R[3]              0.916   0.000  0.010     0.896     0.935 31259 1.000
sd_R[4]              0.902   0.000  0.010     0.883     0.921 33600 1.000
sd_R[5]              0.891   0.000  0.010     0.872     0.910 37002 1.000
sd_year_morph[1]     0.019   0.000  0.010     0.001     0.042  3976 1.002
sd_year_morph[2]     0.207   0.001  0.041     0.139     0.297  5018 1.001
sd_year_morph[3]     0.173   0.000  0.034     0.116     0.250  6459 1.000
sd_year_morph[4]     0.262   0.001  0.054     0.171     0.382  4735 1.000
sd_year_morph[5]     0.254   0.001  0.049     0.172     0.365  4871 1.001
sd_year_repro        0.443   0.001  0.078     0.316     0.621  4296 1.001
sd_year_surv         0.344   0.001  0.069     0.232     0.505  6477 1.000
sd_init_morph[1]     0.160   0.000  0.019     0.128     0.203  3337 1.000
sd_init_morph[2]     0.278   0.001  0.039     0.210     0.365  4960 1.001
sd_init_morph[3]     0.322   0.001  0.046     0.243     0.422  4875 1.000
sd_init_morph[4]     0.341   0.001  0.046     0.261     0.442  4573 1.000
sd_init_morph[5]     0.275   0.001  0.042     0.204     0.365  4956 1.001
lp__             -8920.553   1.288 68.006 -9055.994 -8789.629  2786 1.002

Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Fri Feb 27 15:52:35 2026.
For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at
convergence, Rhat=1).
:::




By the way, it turns out we are removing approximately 61 rows (out of a total of 4424) that do not have a sex value. So the question is whether it is worth it to include them (by finding the sex from a different place)?








```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# 6. RESULTS & DIAGNOSTICS
# ==============================================================================
# out_lv <- readRDS('Output_everything_model.rds')

# Print the parameters specific to the Joint Starter model
print(out_lv,
      pars = c("beta_morph", "beta_repro", "beta_surv",
               "gamma_repro", "gamma_surv", "Lambda", "sd_R", 
               "sd_year_morph", "sd_year_repro", "sd_year_surv", "sd_init_morph", 
               "h2_lv",
               "rho",    # Measurer variance for morphology
               "lp__"),             # Log-posterior (model fit)
      probs = c(0.025, 0.975),
      digits_summary = 3)




# Plot the main loading matrix and shrinkage
library(rstan)

# Use your actual fit object name (e.g., out_joint)
stan_trace(out_lv, pars = c("Lambda", "rho"))

# Look specifically at the Selection Gradients for the 2nd LV
stan_trace(out_lv, pars = c("gamma_repro", "gamma_surv"))

# Plot the main loading matrix and shrinkage

stan_trace(out_lv, pars = c("beta_morph[2,3]", "beta_morph[2,4]", "beta_morph[2,5]", 
                            "beta_morph[3,3]", "beta_morph[3,4]", "beta_morph[3,5]",
                            "beta_morph[4,3]", "beta_morph[4,4]", "beta_morph[4,5]"))



```

Alright, so it mght be an issue that both age and season (as fixed effects) are sturggling against the latent variables for being the source of variance. In any case it seems for the most part that also in the case of the beta's not converging it's mainly chain 4's fault. 

Below are the results of the full run with a bunch of both fixed and random effects and also repro and surv added. It failed. Notice that also some of the beta's are not converged. This could be the result of a "tug-of-war" between the fixed effects and the latent variables, for example age is a fixed effect, but that also accounts for variation in general body size, so having that independent of the LV causes the model to not understand where to put the variation, and that could lead to convergence issues.


Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
4 chains, each with iter=6000; warmup=2000; thin=1; 
post-warmup draws per chain=4000, total post-warmup draws=16000.

                      mean se_mean     sd      2.5%     97.5% n_eff  Rhat
beta_morph[1,1]     -0.032   0.001  0.057    -0.142     0.081  7807 1.000
beta_morph[1,2]      0.208   0.001  0.065     0.081     0.338  4268 1.006
beta_morph[1,3]      0.292   0.001  0.058     0.177     0.407  5281 1.003
beta_morph[1,4]      0.347   0.003  0.077     0.197     0.498   908 1.014
beta_morph[1,5]      0.065   0.001  0.068    -0.067     0.199  5037 1.005
beta_morph[2,1]     -0.002   0.000  0.010    -0.022     0.017 21881 1.001
beta_morph[2,2]     -0.828   0.000  0.026    -0.879    -0.776  6707 1.005
beta_morph[2,3]     -0.946   0.021  0.040    -1.016    -0.863     4 1.423
beta_morph[2,4]     -0.927   0.023  0.042    -0.997    -0.839     3 1.513
beta_morph[2,5]     -0.304   0.013  0.036    -0.373    -0.233     8 1.142
beta_morph[3,1]      0.040   0.000  0.012     0.017     0.063 23049 1.000
beta_morph[3,2]     -0.011   0.000  0.033    -0.075     0.054 10614 1.004
beta_morph[3,3]     -0.013   0.006  0.039    -0.090     0.065    46 1.028
beta_morph[3,4]      0.261   0.006  0.038     0.187     0.337    47 1.027
beta_morph[3,5]      0.250   0.001  0.043     0.166     0.333  1458 1.007
beta_morph[4,1]     -0.005   0.000  0.012    -0.028     0.018 21404 1.000
beta_morph[4,2]      0.402   0.001  0.033     0.336     0.466   571 1.011
beta_morph[4,3]      0.107   0.015  0.044     0.021     0.193     8 1.139
beta_morph[4,4]      0.344   0.018  0.045     0.257     0.429     7 1.186
beta_morph[4,5]      0.321   0.009  0.045     0.232     0.409    23 1.050
beta_repro[1]       -0.186   0.002  0.100    -0.388     0.008  3870 1.000
beta_repro[2]       -0.411   0.000  0.048    -0.506    -0.315 27105 1.000
beta_surv[1]         0.096   0.001  0.094    -0.087     0.283  9311 1.000
beta_surv[2]         0.193   0.000  0.081     0.035     0.352 26845 1.000
gamma_repro[1]       0.059   0.000  0.025     0.011     0.107 22693 1.000
gamma_repro[2]       0.042   0.008  0.033    -0.024     0.104    18 1.063
gamma_surv[1]        0.123   0.000  0.043     0.038     0.206 10126 1.000
gamma_surv[2]        0.122   0.012  0.053     0.022     0.229    20 1.057
Lambda[1,1]          0.969   0.000  0.020     0.930     1.010  3029 1.002
Lambda[1,2]         -0.018   0.002  0.064    -0.151     0.109   874 1.002
Lambda[2,1]          0.226   0.001  0.041     0.146     0.307  1126 1.005
Lambda[2,2]          0.589   0.071  0.103     0.378     0.689     2 4.458
Lambda[3,1]          0.166   0.001  0.028     0.109     0.225   747 1.007
Lambda[3,2]          0.307   0.070  0.100     0.210     0.508     2 4.697
Lambda[4,1]          0.121   0.001  0.031     0.058     0.187   743 1.007
Lambda[4,2]          0.361   0.074  0.106     0.262     0.569     2 5.080
Lambda[5,1]          0.311   0.001  0.026     0.258     0.363   983 1.005
Lambda[5,2]          0.297   0.034  0.055     0.221     0.420     3 2.065
sd_R[1]              0.187   0.000  0.003     0.182     0.192  9389 1.000
sd_R[2]              0.586   0.058  0.083     0.518     0.741     2 7.307
sd_R[3]              0.752   0.026  0.037     0.677     0.789     2 4.158
sd_R[4]              0.726   0.032  0.046     0.635     0.771     2 5.081
sd_R[5]              0.835   0.009  0.015     0.802     0.859     3 1.651
sd_year_morph[1]     0.016   0.000  0.010     0.001     0.038  4992 1.000
sd_year_morph[2]     0.128   0.001  0.035     0.072     0.207  3849 1.006
sd_year_morph[3]     0.125   0.003  0.028     0.077     0.188    91 1.022
sd_year_morph[4]     0.234   0.013  0.059     0.139     0.369    20 1.067
sd_year_morph[5]     0.193   0.002  0.042     0.123     0.287   388 1.015
sd_year_repro        0.442   0.001  0.076     0.315     0.610  5345 1.001
sd_year_surv         0.347   0.001  0.070     0.232     0.503  7126 1.000
sd_init_morph[1]     0.156   0.000  0.019     0.124     0.198  4464 1.000
sd_init_morph[2]     0.265   0.001  0.035     0.206     0.343  2671 1.008
sd_init_morph[3]     0.278   0.001  0.039     0.211     0.363  1323 1.011
sd_init_morph[4]     0.318   0.001  0.041     0.247     0.409  5754 1.001
sd_init_morph[5]     0.268   0.001  0.043     0.193     0.361  5383 1.005
h2_lv[1]             0.471   0.001  0.046     0.381     0.560  1989 1.001
h2_lv[2]             0.542   0.016  0.065     0.415     0.670    16 1.093
rho                  0.158   0.030  0.113     0.031     0.456    14 1.084
lp__             -7583.915   1.492 91.523 -7765.291 -7409.708  3762 1.003

Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Thu Mar  5 05:51:39 2026.
For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at 
convergence, Rhat=1).



Now testing what happens if I remove the one chain that disagreed a lot with the others, chain 4. 
But that isn't really good practice to remove one chain after the fact, but yeah: 

```{r}
library(rstan)



post_array <- as.array(out_lv)

 # 2. Subset the array to keep only chains 2, 3, and 4
 # The second dimension of the array is the chain index
 array_subset <- post_array[, 1:3, ]
 
 
 new_summary <- monitor(array_subset, digits_summary = 3, print = FALSE)
 
 # 2. Convert to a data frame or matrix to enable row-subsetting
 new_summary_df <- as.data.frame(new_summary)
 
 

 # 3. Use rstan's monitor function to generate a new summary table
 # This will recalculate Mean, SD, n_eff, and R-hat for the remaining chains
 
 # 4. View the specific parameters that were "messy" before
 pars_to_check <- c("gamma_repro[1]", "gamma_repro[2]", 
                    "gamma_surv[1]", "gamma_surv[2]", 
                    "Lambda[1,1]", "Lambda[1,2]", 
                    "Lambda[2,1]", "Lambda[2,2]", 
                    "rho", "h2_lv[2]")
 
 # 3. Print your parameters of interest
 print(new_summary_df[pars_to_check, ])
```


                      mean      se_mean         sd         2.5%         25%         50%        75%
gamma_repro[1]  0.05876032 0.0002661359 0.02524820  0.009481862  0.04175122  0.05915998 0.07640587
gamma_repro[2]  0.04777510 0.0003172129 0.02986328 -0.010871722  0.02730378  0.04818482 0.06733020
gamma_surv[1]   0.12208226 0.0004901230 0.04279028  0.037763686  0.09338545  0.12184798 0.15100881
gamma_surv[2]   0.11208502 0.0005623019 0.04955137  0.014866939  0.07866373  0.11199741 0.14616805
Lambda[1,1]     0.96976553 0.0005421117 0.02003019  0.931859582  0.95627350  0.96941554 0.98287756
Lambda[1,2]    -0.01921731 0.0023323181 0.05477292 -0.142738853 -0.04933346 -0.01567230 0.01635498
Lambda[2,1]     0.22380085 0.0016100168 0.03987824  0.149546045  0.19797763  0.22217643 0.24814697
Lambda[2,2]     0.64693420 0.0007073727 0.02299174  0.600572777  0.63206784  0.64742157 0.66219629
rho             0.13362541 0.0023481142 0.09816247  0.027553851  0.06450410  0.10327557 0.17425838
h2_lv[2]        0.55484251 0.0023513491 0.06180481  0.434449028  0.51157360  0.55515390 0.59773809
                    97.5% n_eff      Rhat valid            Q5         Q50        Q95    MCSE_Q2.5
gamma_repro[1] 0.10700486  8802 1.0004085     1  0.0163985275  0.05915998 0.09930538 0.0009446112
gamma_repro[2] 0.10743334  8826 1.0002299     1 -0.0009635907  0.04818482 0.09638510 0.0010513245
gamma_surv[1]  0.20544156  7597 1.0002403     1  0.0525235148  0.12184798 0.19318054 0.0017109337
gamma_surv[2]  0.20727535  7694 0.9998258     1  0.0305582623  0.11199741 0.19381430 0.0023681569
Lambda[1,1]    1.01051147  1352 1.0011862     1  0.9374574698  0.96941554 1.00355302 0.0009286183
Lambda[1,2]    0.07991625   550 1.0040894     1 -0.1159916319 -0.01567230 0.06274527 0.0065584238
Lambda[2,1]    0.30945131   612 1.0029961     1  0.1610302094  0.22217643 0.29281746 0.0025564566
Lambda[2,2]    0.69168214  1046 1.0016785     1  0.6091233641  0.64742157 0.68412126 0.0020906611
rho            0.40826256  1734 1.0013450     1  0.0337616989  0.10327557 0.34081387 0.0010535992
h2_lv[2]       0.67290882   682 1.0017496     1  0.4520669502  0.55515390 0.65596094 0.0028583820
                   MCSE_Q25     MCSE_Q50     MCSE_Q75   MCSE_Q97.5      MCSE_SD Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
gamma_repro[1] 0.0004963538 0.0004024525 0.0004945841 0.0009398345 0.0002032631     8982     4495
gamma_repro[2] 0.0005834702 0.0003947184 0.0005480688 0.0014949426 0.0002697446     8902     4460
gamma_surv[1]  0.0009158824 0.0005036031 0.0007810828 0.0013057482 0.0003705827     7607     4188
gamma_surv[2]  0.0008147991 0.0007712307 0.0010868857 0.0016850624 0.0004293244     7771     4687
Lambda[1,1]    0.0005748420 0.0005895095 0.0005358934 0.0011162593 0.0003834128     1365     3118
Lambda[1,2]    0.0026418117 0.0018097028 0.0018903110 0.0045931769 0.0016500709      575      739
Lambda[2,1]    0.0014175238 0.0013694119 0.0018220951 0.0048068249 0.0011508057      627      915
Lambda[2,2]    0.0008354725 0.0007201968 0.0007976987 0.0011090193 0.0005003262     1065     1768
rho            0.0013674191 0.0019084002 0.0035337562 0.0085709880 0.0016606446     1616     2778
h2_lv[2]       0.0029986225 0.0024574171 0.0029831721 0.0033503018 0.0016671106      692     1601





```{r}
# 1. Extract the 3D array from your Stan fit
post_array <- as.array(out_lv)

# 2. Define a quick function to get the Mean and 95% CI
summarize_chain <- function(x) {
  c(Mean = mean(x), 
    `2.5%` = quantile(x, 0.025), 
    `97.5%` = quantile(x, 0.975))
}

# 3. Apply the function to the lp__ parameter across the chains (dimension 2)
cat("\n--- lp__ Summary Per Chain ---\n")
lp_summary <- apply(post_array[, , "lp__"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(lp_summary, 2))

# 4. Do the same for rho to see exactly how Chain 4 differs numerically
cat("\n--- rho Summary Per Chain ---\n")
rho_summary <- apply(post_array[, , "rho"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(rho_summary, 3))
```
Clearly significantly lower for the 4th chain on the lp, suggesting it has reached a local minimum. 


Below is code to show denisty for rho for the different chains, along with some more diagnostic plots of the log posterior. 
```{r}

# Load the required libraries
library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Convert your Stan fit object to an array (bayesplot prefers this format)
# Replace 'out_joint' with whatever you named your loaded .rds model
posterior_array <- as.array(out_lv)

# 2. Plot the overlaid densities for each chain
mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array, pars = c("rho")) +
  ggtitle("Posterior Density of rho (Shrinkage Factor) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Optional: If you also want to see the combined density of just the "good" chains
# (assuming chains 2, 3, and 4 are the converged ones)
mcmc_dens(posterior_array[, 2:4, ], pars = c("rho")) +
  ggtitle("Combined Posterior Density of rho (Chains 2-4 only)") +
  theme_minimal()





# Assuming your array is loaded as 'posterior_array'
# (e.g., posterior_array <- as.array(out_joint))

# Plot 1: The Elevation Trace
# Are all chains at the same height?
mcmc_trace(posterior_array, pars = "lp__") +
  ggtitle("Traceplot of Log-Posterior (lp__) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Plot 2: The Elevation Density
# Do the distributions perfectly overlap, or is one shifted left (lower)?
mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array, pars = "lp__") +
  ggtitle("Density of Log-Posterior (lp__) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Plot 3: The Scatterplot (Bob's suggestion)
# This maps the GPS coordinate (rho) against the Elevation (lp__)
# If you see two distinct clouds, you have a multimodal posterior!
mcmc_scatter(posterior_array, pars = c("rho", "lp__")) +
  ggtitle("Geometry of the Posterior: rho vs lp__") +
  theme_minimal()
```



Now I'll check the results for the first model that didn't work for 2 latent variables on both morphology and fitness. This is from the R script combined_test_model.R (or that script might actually contain the 1 lv version which i did afterwards...). What I need to check is the chains and also the rho densities and also the log posteriors, and maybe more. 

```{r}
out_old <- readRDS('output_markov/Output_Joint_Starter.rds')

print(out_old,
      # pars = c(               "gamma_repro",       # Selection gradient: Size -> Reproduction!
      #          "gamma_surv",        # Selection gradient: Size -> Survival!
      #          "Lambda",            # Factor loadings (How traits build Size)
      #          "h2_lv",             # Heritability of the Latent Size
      #          "rho",               # Shrinkage factor
      #          "sd_R",              # Residual error for morph traits
      #          "sd_year_morph",     # Year variance for morphology
      #          "sd_year_repro",     # Year variance for reproduction
      #          "sd_year_surv",      # Year variance for survival
      #          "sd_init_morph",     # Measurer variance for morphology
      #          "lp__"),             # Log-posterior (model fit)
      probs = c(0.025, 0.975),
      digits_summary = 3)
```



```{r}
library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Plot a stable parameter (The Grand Mean)
color_scheme_set("mix-blue-red")
p1 <- mcmc_trace(out_old, pars = c("alpha_trait[1]", "sd_year_surv")) +
  ggtitle("Stable Parameters (Rhat = 1.00)")

# 2. Plot the 'Flipped' parameters
p2 <- mcmc_trace(out_old, pars = c("Lambda[1,1]", "gamma_repro[1]", "gamma_surv[1]")) +
  ggtitle("Sign-Flipped Parameters (High Rhat)")

p3 <- mcmc_trace(out_old, pars = c("Lambda[1,2]", "Lambda[3,2]", "gamma_repro[2]", "gamma_surv[2]"))

p4 <- mcmc_trace(out_old, pars = c("Lambda[2,1]", "Lambda[1,2]", "Lambda[3,1]", "Lambda[2,2]"))

  

# Show the plots
print(p1)
print(p2)
print(p3)
print(p4)
```
```{r}

# Load the required libraries
library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Convert your Stan fit object to an array (bayesplot prefers this format)
# Replace 'out_joint' with whatever you named your loaded .rds model
posterior_array <- as.array(out_old)

# 2. Plot the overlaid densities for each chain
mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array, pars = c("rho")) +
  ggtitle("Posterior Density of rho (Shrinkage Factor) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Optional: If you also want to see the combined density of just the "good" chains
# (assuming chains 2, 3, and 4 are the converged ones)
mcmc_dens(posterior_array[, 2:4, ], pars = c("rho")) +
  ggtitle("Combined Posterior Density of rho (Chains 2-4 only)") +
  theme_minimal()





# Assuming your array is loaded as 'posterior_array'
# (e.g., posterior_array <- as.array(out_joint))

# Plot 1: The Elevation Trace
# Are all chains at the same height?
mcmc_trace(posterior_array, pars = "lp__") +
  ggtitle("Traceplot of Log-Posterior (lp__) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Plot 2: The Elevation Density
# Do the distributions perfectly overlap, or is one shifted left (lower)?
mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array, pars = "lp__") +
  ggtitle("Density of Log-Posterior (lp__) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

# Plot 3: The Scatterplot (Bob's suggestion)
# This maps the GPS coordinate (rho) against the Elevation (lp__)
# If you see two distinct clouds, you have a multimodal posterior!
mcmc_scatter(posterior_array, pars = c("rho", "lp__")) +
  ggtitle("Geometry of the Posterior: rho vs lp__") +
  theme_minimal()


# 1. Extract the 3D array from your Stan fit

# 2. Define a quick function to get the Mean and 95% CI
summarize_chain <- function(x) {
  c(Mean = mean(x), 
    `2.5%` = quantile(x, 0.025), 
    `97.5%` = quantile(x, 0.975))
}

# 3. Apply the function to the lp__ parameter across the chains (dimension 2)
cat("\n--- lp__ Summary Per Chain ---\n")
lp_summary <- apply(posterior_array[, , "lp__"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(lp_summary, 2))

# 4. Do the same for rho to see exactly how Chain 4 differs numerically
cat("\n--- rho Summary Per Chain ---\n")
rho_summary <- apply(posterior_array[, , "rho"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(rho_summary, 3))

```
So, it looks like, contrary to the latest run, this older run actually had two equally good modes, as you can see that the posterior mean for the lp for chain 1 (doing its own thing) is almost exactly the same as the others. 


Here are the results for the latest run. This is a run where I put a beta(1,2) prior on rho. And I didn't include that many fixed effects (or really none). The script is the one called something with "hopefully". 
```{r}
out_hopeful <- readRDS("output_markov/Output_hopefully.rds")
print(out_hopeful, probs = c(0.025, 0.975), digits_summary = 3)
``` 
Now, this looks like some issues with mixing again, so we need to investigate. It seems the betas and the residuals are looking fine though, the and rho is also looking okay, so it might just be a case of sign flipping (although that shouldn't be possible anymore). So we need to look at the chains. 

```{r}
library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)

color_scheme_set("mix-blue-red")

chains1 <- mcmc_trace(out_hopeful, pars = c("Lambda[1,1]", "Lambda[1,2]", "Lambda[3,1]", "Lambda[3,2]"))
chains2 <- mcmc_trace(out_hopeful, pars = c("gamma_repro[1]", "gamma_repro[2]", "gamma_surv[1]", "gamma_surv[2]", "rho"))
chains3 <- mcmc_trace(out_hopeful, pars = c("Lambda[2,1]", "Lambda[2,2]", "Lambda[4,1]", "Lambda[4,2]", "Lambda[5,1]", "Lambda[5,2]"))
chains4 <- mcmc_trace(out_hopeful, pars= c("lp__", "h2_lv[1]", "h2_lv[2]"))

print(chains1)
print(chains2)
print(chains3)
print(chains4)



```

Alright, it looks like there are two possible modes that have multiple chains each. So we need to check some densities and log posteriors. 

```{r}  
posterior_array_2 <- as.array(out_hopeful)

mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array_2, pars = c("rho")) +
  ggtitle("Posterior Density of rho (Shrinkage Factor) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()

```

Alright it looks like there are 3 chains in each case, and they seem to slightly disagree on the distribution of rho.

```{r}
mcmc_dens_overlay(posterior_array_2, pars = "lp__") +
  ggtitle("Density of Log-Posterior (lp__) per Chain") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Okay, so the log posteriors seem to be slightly different for the two modes. 

```{r}
summarize_chain <- function(x) {
  c(Mean = mean(x), 
    `2.5%` = quantile(x, 0.025), 
    `97.5%` = quantile(x, 0.975))
}

# 3. Apply the function to the lp__ parameter across the chains (dimension 2)
cat("\n--- lp__ Summary Per Chain ---\n")
lp_summary <- apply(posterior_array_2[, , "lp__"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(lp_summary, 2))

# 4. Do the same for rho to see exactly how Chain 4 differs numerically
cat("\n--- rho Summary Per Chain ---\n")
rho_summary <- apply(posterior_array_2[, , "rho"], MARGIN = 2, FUN = summarize_chain)
print(round(rho_summary, 3))
```

It seems the mode with rho around 0.28 has somewhat higher log posteriors, by approximately 5 log units, compared to the mode with rho around 0.34-0.35, and 5 log units is a significant difference. But the differences within each mode is also quite large, so the 5 log unit difference is quite fuzzy. 

Now, one question is, can I just take the log posterior and say that, hey, this mode has a significantly higher log posterior, so this is the better mode, and then ignore the other one? If not, how do I prevent this duality in the run? Should I just tweak the priors even more or the initialization to avoid the model from going into the "bad" mode?

But in any case, it seems the model finds evidence for a second latent variable for these data, although that isn't exactly shown without a regularization or something, I suppose. 

What is interesting is that even though the model seems to respect the rho, the loadings seem to be about the same magnitude in both modes. If the hypothesis that the body size is the latent variable representing the most variance, it would not make sense that the rho would be only about 0.3 if the first LV represents shape in one of the modes. Then the rho would have to be like 3 or something. One possibility is that it is the lambda_raw that gets blown up, to accommodate the requirement for both rho and the actual loadings. But the lambda_raw has a standard normal prior (I think), this might need to be tightened, or I'm not exactly sure what can be done except constraining some loadings.
